title: And Maggie Makes Three

jokes.text: Don't have to yell, Michael. I'm all around you.
jokes.setup: Michael yells at the boat to go faster
jokes.punchline: The boat responds conversationally, breaking the fourth wall about being 'all around' the character
jokes.explanation: Strong absurdist humor - a crime-solving boat that's self-aware and conversational. Good character integration for this fictional TV show within the show.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Go faster!

dialogue.speaker: Knightboat
dialogue.line: I'm all around you, Michael.

jokes.text: Every week, there's a canal. Or an inlet. Or a fjord.
jokes.setup: The boat finds a convenient waterway to continue the chase
jokes.punchline: Characters point out the ridiculous convenience of always having water access
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons meta-humor, pointing out the absurd convenience of TV show logic. Very economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Wow, a river! Just when we needed it most!

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: It's like the writers knew exactly what we needed.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: The narrative convenience is almost too obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, we are in Springfield. There's always water around when the plot needs it.

jokes.text: Quiet! I won't hear another word against the boat.
jokes.setup: Family members criticize the ridiculous TV show
jokes.punchline: Homer defends the obviously flawed show with passionate intensity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - defending something absurd with complete sincerity. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, hey! Don't you criticize my show! This program has won seventeen Emmys!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's a completely ridiculous show.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So? It's on TV, isn't it? If it's dumb enough to be on TV, it's smart enough for me!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, the plot makes no sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, just 'cause I don't care doesn't mean I'm not listening.

jokes.text: Says you, woman.
jokes.setup: Marge says the boat sets a bad example
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive, outdated response
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer casual sexism played for character comedy. Economical but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, a boat's a boat. It's not going to teach Bart anything except maybe how to have fun. And what's wrong with that?

jokes.text: It was Bart.
jokes.setup: Homer is clearly still watching TV when confronted
jokes.punchline: Immediately blames Bart without any setup
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer deflection behavior - blame the kids immediately. Very economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart did it!

jokes.text: And here's our TV next to the mirror. It looks like we have two.
jokes.setup: Looking at family photos
jokes.punchline: Marge points out an optical illusion that creates the appearance of luxury
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag about working-class aspirations and making the best of what you have. Character-appropriate for Marge.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Look at this photo from our trip to Capital City. See how the angle makes our car look like a Mercedes?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, we're rich!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's just an optical illusion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An optical illusion of being rich is still better than our actual life.

jokes.text: Two. Two. Yeah, yeah, two. Let's get to the baby pictures.
jokes.setup: Family is impressed by the 'two TVs'
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissively rushes past his wife's moment of pride
jokes.explanation: Homer's impatience and lack of social awareness. Good character moment but not exceptional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Two TVs? Big deal. I had two TVs back in '82.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, one of 'em was a brick.

jokes.text: Here's Bart sleeping. Here he is dozing. Here he is after a visit from the sandman. Here's nappy time, Bart. Here's a cute one. He's all tuckered out.
jokes.setup: Looking at baby pictures of Bart
jokes.punchline: Marge has multiple redundant photos of the same thing with different euphemisms for sleeping
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke about parental over-documentation. Perfect Marge behavior - earnest but excessive. Strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Look at all these pictures of Bart napping... napping... resting his eyes... taking a little snooze... catching forty winks... getting some shut-eye...

jokes.text: Bart, you were Lisa's age. And, Lisa... you were the age Bart was several years ago.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining the timeline of the story
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily convoluted way to say they were younger
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about unnecessarily complex explanations. Character-appropriate for Marge's earnestness.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We were younger then.

jokes.text: It was a tumultuous time for our nation. The clear-beverage craze gave us all a reason to live.
jokes.setup: Setting the historical context
jokes.punchline: Treating a trivial consumer trend as existentially important
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons social satire - mocking how consumer trends are treated as cultural milestones. Very quotable and well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose every generation has its little fads. When I was young, it was Madge pottery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, this is different. This is the end of civilization as we know it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's just a new flavor of Squishee.

jokes.text: The information superhighway showed the average person... what some nerd thinks about Star Trek.
jokes.setup: Describing the early internet era
jokes.punchline: Reducing the entire internet to Star Trek nerd opinions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant prescient satire of early internet culture. Extremely economical and accurate prediction of online discourse.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The early internet was just Star Trek nerd opinions about everything.

jokes.text: And the domestication of the dog continued unabated.
jokes.setup: Listing major cultural developments of the era
jokes.punchline: Treating dog domestication (which happened thousands of years ago) as current news
jokes.explanation: Subtle absurdist humor about treating ancient history as current events. Good deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I just heard the most wonderful news on the radio today. They're saying that dogs have been domesticated!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Domesticated? What do you mean, Marge?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Apparently, it just happened! They're calling it a major cultural development of our time.

jokes.text: Not on my shift!
jokes.setup: Terrorists announce they've taken over the plant
jokes.punchline: Homer's overconfident response to overwhelming odds
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer bravado in the face of impossible odds. Very economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll stop them. I've stopped worse than this.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Remember that time I fought that bear? Or when I took on the whole town in dodgeball?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Those terrorists don't stand a chance against Homer Simpson.

jokes.text: Simpson, 10, terrorists, 8.
jokes.setup: Homer fighting terrorists single-handedly
jokes.punchline: Treating a life-or-death battle like a sports score
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - reduces complex action sequence to simple scoreboard. Good visual comedy setup.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Take that, terrorists! And that! Oh, I'm winning 7 to 3!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, they scored... 7 to 5. Aww, come on!

jokes.text: I don't think a healthy man can make that kind of smell.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently makes a terrible smell
jokes.punchline: Coworker's clinical assessment of Homer's bodily functions
jokes.explanation: Basic bodily humor. Functional but not particularly creative or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Good Lord, what is happening in there?

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Fire! Fire!

jokes.text: With a single glorious check... I'm finally completely out of debt.
jokes.setup: Homer receiving his paycheck
jokes.punchline: The grandiose language for basic financial responsibility
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's tendency to dramatize mundane achievements.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Paycheck!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, you gonna save some of that?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Save? That's just fancy talk for 'not spending.'

jokes.text: Yes! With my bills paid off, I can finally quit this lousy job.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating paying off debt
jokes.punchline: Immediately deciding to quit his income source
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - solving one problem by creating a bigger one. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I paid off all my debt! I'm free!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm quitting my job at the nuclear plant.

jokes.text: Don't worry about Homer J. I've got a plan. A plan that'll fix you good.
jokes.setup: Homer reassuring his coworkers about his plan
jokes.punchline: Accidentally threatening them instead of reassuring
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Homer's verbal confusion leading to unintended threats. Classic malapropism.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, guys. I've got a plan that'll make us all rich.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All you have to do is exactly what I say, when I say it, or I'll know where you live.

jokes.text: Lovely desk. It would be too bad if someone... oh, I don't know, didn't use a coaster!
jokes.setup: Homer threatening to vandalize Mr. Burns' office
jokes.punchline: The 'vandalism' is extremely mild office discourtesy
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - builds up vandalism tension then deflates with incredibly mild transgression. Great structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go vandalize Mr. Burns' office!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna rearrange his desk supplies and not ask permission!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And then I'm gonna use his stapler without putting it back in the exact same spot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, and I might not even refill the tape dispenser when it runs out!

jokes.text: Such a nice, tidy office. Maybe it's time you started living like a pig!
jokes.setup: Homer continuing his 'vandalism' spree
jokes.punchline: The dramatic announcement followed by presumably mild mess-making
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the coaster bit. Maintains the pattern of dramatic buildup for mild transgression.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going on a vandalism spree!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to commit the most heinous acts of destruction known to man!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to... deface this stop sign!

jokes.text: Oh, I hope I haven't upset you... bongo head!
jokes.setup: Homer's fake concern for Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: The incredibly mild insult 'bongo head'
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the mild rebellion theme. 'Bongo head' is perfectly absurd and quotable while being completely harmless.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, sir, I'm worried about you. You look terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Worried? About me? That's very touching, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you've been working too hard. You need to take it easy, you bongo head.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bongo head?

jokes.text: Oh, I should be resisting this, but I'm paralyzed with rage... and island rhythms.
jokes.setup: Homer is apparently playing Burns' head like bongos
jokes.punchline: Burns admits to enjoying the musical assault
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist humor. Burns' clinical self-awareness about his own paralysis is perfect character work. The 'island rhythms' detail elevates it significantly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bong bong bong bong bong!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I'm enjoying this. Keep it up!

jokes.text: Go, Homer. Yeah. Yeah. Way to play the boss's head like a bongo, Homer. He's getting a pretty good sound out of that guy.
jokes.setup: Homer playing Burns' head like bongos
jokes.punchline: Coworkers providing color commentary like it's a musical performance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the absurd situation. The musical critique angle adds another layer to the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Oh, beautiful tone!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Listen to that resonance.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: He's really got a way with the old skull.

jokes.text: Barney, any chance you can get me a job? Hey. Sure, Homer. I told you, my uncle owns the place.
jokes.setup: Homer's 'final phase' of his master plan
jokes.punchline: His plan is just asking his drunk friend for a job
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Homer's grandiose 'plan.' Perfect character work showing his lack of actual planning.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And now for the final phase of my master plan...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Barney, will you give me a job?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Sure, Homer!

jokes.text: Hey, Uncle Al, can Homer have a job? Sure. Barney, you're fired. Okay.
jokes.setup: Barney asking for Homer to get a job
jokes.punchline: Uncle Al fires Barney to make room, Barney accepts it casually
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy. Barney's casual acceptance of being fired is perfect character work. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, you gotta get a job so I can have yours.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: You're fired, Barney.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Okay!

jokes.text: Show up tomorrow. Bring three rags. Oh, and a change of pants. Why? When it happens, you'll know.
jokes.setup: Uncle Al giving Homer job instructions
jokes.punchline: Ominous warning about pants-soiling incident
jokes.explanation: Good ominous setup for future embarrassment. The vagueness makes it funnier and more quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: Listen Homer, I'm warning you - don't mess this up or you'll end up like the last guy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What happened to the last guy?

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: Let's just say he left here with more than he came in with... if you know what I mean.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He got a raise?

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: No, a pants-soiling incident that'll haunt him for the rest of his life.

jokes.text: You're a pin monkey? Finally, I don't have to be ashamed of my father's job.
jokes.setup: Homer got a job at a bowling alley
jokes.punchline: Bart thinks being a 'pin monkey' is more prestigious than nuclear safety
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony - Bart prefers his dad being a 'pin monkey' over nuclear safety inspector. Great character insight and social commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're a pin monkey? That's so cool! Way more prestigious than working at a nuclear power plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, boy. I'm living the dream.

jokes.text: From now on, we use regular toilet paper... not that fancy quilted kind.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining their new budget
jokes.punchline: Treating quilted toilet paper as a major luxury
jokes.explanation: Good class commentary - what constitutes luxury for the Simpson family. Character-appropriate penny-pinching.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're on a tight budget now, so we have to cut back on luxuries.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No more quilted toilet paper. We're going back to the single-ply.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, quilted toilet paper isn't a luxury.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not a luxury? Marge, do you know what quilted means? It's fancy!

jokes.text: And only one of you kids can go to college. Fine. Fine.
jokes.setup: Homer announcing budget cuts
jokes.punchline: Casually limiting college to one child, kids accept it immediately
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor about economic reality. The kids' casual acceptance makes it funnier and more disturbing. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright, family, I have some bad news. Budget cuts mean I can only afford to send one of you to college.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, I'm out.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I suppose that's fair.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Great! Problem solved!

jokes.text: I've got sand in my underpants. Me too. Let's go home.
jokes.setup: Romantic beach stroll
jokes.punchline: Romance immediately killed by uncomfortable sand
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of romantic moment with mundane discomfort. Economical but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, isn't this romantic? Just you, me, and the sound of the waves.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's beautiful, Homie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ugh! Sand in my pants! Sand in my shirt! Sand in places sand should never be!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're ruining the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm ruining it? The beach is ruining it! Who decided to put all this sand here?

jokes.text: Everything in our lives is finally perfectly balanced. I hope things stay exactly like this forever.
jokes.setup: Homer expressing contentment
jokes.punchline: Famous last words before everything changes with the pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony - we know everything is about to change. Good setup for the pregnancy revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Everything's perfect. I got a job, a wife, a son. I couldn't ask for anything more.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Did you have to be so graphic? It's okay, Marge. They pave the way for this kind of filth in school.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently told the conception story too explicitly
jokes.punchline: Homer blames schools for corrupting the kids
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Homer deflecting blame for his own inappropriate storytelling. Classic parental hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why would you tell them that?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just telling them the truth about where babies come from.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: They're five years old!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if the schools would do their job and teach this stuff, I wouldn't have to!

jokes.text: Hey, Homer. Hi there, Homer. Hi, Homer. Hi there, Homer. Hi.
jokes.setup: Homer greeting his coworkers
jokes.punchline: Everyone has the exact same generic greeting pattern
jokes.explanation: Minor observational humor about workplace monotony. Functional but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey guys, how's it going?

dialogue.speaker: Bowling alley worker 1
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, how's it going?

dialogue.speaker: Bowling alley worker 2
dialogue.line: Homer! How's it going?

dialogue.speaker: Bowling alley worker 3
dialogue.line: How's it going, Homer?

jokes.text: Bowling-fresh.
jokes.setup: Homer working at the bowling alley
jokes.punchline: Making up advertising-speak for bowling equipment
jokes.explanation: Good parody of advertising language. Very economical and fits Homer's simple wordplay style.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Bowling Alley! We've got shoes so comfortable, you'll forget you're wearing shoes. Bowling balls so round, you'll wonder if they're actually round. And pins so... pinny, you'll want to knock 'em down!

jokes.text: Urinal-fresh.
jokes.setup: Homer continuing his made-up advertising terms
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly inappropriate bathroom references
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from the previous joke, though relies somewhat on bathroom humor. Still economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I call the big one 'Bitey.' Now that's advertising!

jokes.text: Homer, did you polish your head in the Shine-O Ball-O? No. Okay, then.
jokes.setup: Homer's head is apparently very shiny
jokes.punchline: Coworker's casual inquiry about head-polishing equipment
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy about Homer's baldness. The matter-of-fact delivery and specific equipment name make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, where'd you get your head polished?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I didn't polish it. It's just naturally shiny.

jokes.text: If horseracing is the sport of kings... then surely, bowling... is a very good sport as well.
jokes.setup: Homer beginning a grandiose comparison
jokes.punchline: Completely deflating the comparison with underwhelming praise
jokes.explanation: Exceptional misdirection joke. Sets up grandiose comparison then completely deflates it. Perfect Homer logic and highly quotable structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm like a modern-day Leonardo da Vinci.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Except I can't paint, sculpt, or invent anything. But I'm really good at eating donuts.

jokes.text: Hey, great job, Homer. Thanks a lot, Homer. Hey, you're the best. I'm gonna make it after all
jokes.setup: Homer getting praised at his new job
jokes.punchline: Homer channeling Mary Tyler Moore's iconic moment for his bowling alley job
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference parody. The contrast between Mary Tyler Moore's career success and Homer's bowling job creates the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna make it after all!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *throws hat in the air*

jokes.text: Well, you know, a healthy baby can bring upwards of $60,000. What?! Of course, that was just a test. Had you reacted differently, why, you'd be in jail right now. Simply a test.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert casually mentioning baby selling
jokes.punchline: Claiming it was a 'test' after Marge's shocked reaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor. Dr. Hibbert's casual mention of baby selling followed by the obvious lie about it being a 'test' is perfectly crafted and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I may have mentioned something about selling babies, but that was just a test.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: A test?!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: To see if you were paying attention.

jokes.text: Been there, done that. I hope it's a girl. You know nothing about genetics, Lis. It goes boy, girl, boy, girl.
jokes.setup: Lisa hoping for a girl baby
jokes.punchline: Bart's completely wrong understanding of how genetics works
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - confident in completely wrong information. Good sibling dynamics and child logic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I hope the baby is a girl. That way I'll have a sister.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? The baby's gonna be a boy. I'm gonna make sure of it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's not how genetics works.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well I've been doing special exercises. I flex my man muscles and think boy thoughts. It's science.

jokes.text: Dear Lord, the gods have been good to me, and I am thankful.
jokes.setup: Homer praying
jokes.punchline: Mixing Christian and polytheistic religious references
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer religious confusion. Good character work showing his muddled understanding of theology.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dear Lord, we paid for all this stuff ourselves, so thanks for nothing.

jokes.text: If that is okay, please give me absolutely no sign. Okay, deal. In gratitude, I present you this offering of cookies and milk. If you want me to eat them for you, give me no sign. Thy will be done.
jokes.setup: Homer asking God not to change anything
jokes.punchline: Homer manipulating divine silence to justify eating the cookies
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - using religious framework to justify gluttony. Perfect character integration and brilliant escalation from sincere prayer to cookie theft.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dear God, I'm not asking for much. I just want things to stay exactly as they are right now, forever.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: If you don't want me to eat these cookies, give me a sign.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm waiting... still waiting... I guess that's a yes!

jokes.text: Hello. Is this A. Aaronson? It might interest you to know that Marge Simpson is pregnant again.
jokes.setup: Bart promising not to tell Homer about the pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Immediately starting to spread gossip through the town
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart mischief - technically keeping his promise to not tell Homer while still spreading the news. Great character work.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Dad. I won't tell anyone about the pregnancy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks, son. I'm counting on you.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Milhouse, you won't believe what I just heard...

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Homer's gonna have another baby!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: No way!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Did someone say Homer's having a baby?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: That's the best news I've heard all week!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I'm telling everyone at the plant!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson's gonna be a father again? I'm putting this on the news ticker!

jokes.text: Aaronson and Zowkowski are the two biggest gossips in town. In an hour, everyone will know.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining his gossip strategy
jokes.punchline: Bart has detailed knowledge of the town's gossip network
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Bart as strategic troublemaker with deep knowledge of social dynamics. Shows his intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I've got the whole gossip network mapped out. Moe's bar is the hub, Nelson spreads rumors at school, and my mom tells everyone at book club.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: By Thursday, what started as me mentioning Lisa's weird science project is somehow the town scandal.

jokes.text: It's true, the bundle is little... but I'm not in it for the money.
jokes.setup: Someone congratulating Homer on the 'little bundle of joy'
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks they're talking about his bowling job, not the baby
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection as Homer completely misunderstands the congratulations. Shows his obliviousness and creates dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A little bundle of joy? Oh boy, you heard about my job at the bowling alley!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, they're talking about the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: This is getting very abstract, but thank you... I do enjoy working at the bowling alley.
jokes.setup: More people congratulating Homer about the pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Homer's continued confusion and his comment about it being 'abstract'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's confusion. His use of 'abstract' shows his attempt to intellectualize his bewilderment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. So the baby's gonna come out of where?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that's abstract. I like abstract.

jokes.text: Man, it's windy as hell out there. Hey, wait a minute. What are all these presents? It looks like you're... showering Marge with gifts. With little, tiny baby-sized gifts.
jokes.setup: Homer walking into the baby shower
jokes.punchline: Homer slowly realizing what's happening through observation
jokes.explanation: Good build-up to the revelation. Homer's slow deductive reasoning creates suspense and character-appropriate discovery process.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is there a big banner that says 'Baby Shower'? And why are all these women here? And why is Marge wearing that weird outfit with all the ribbons?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... is this... oh my God, we're having a baby!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why didn't anyone tell me?!

jokes.text: Well, I'll be in the tub.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing Marge is pregnant at the baby shower
jokes.punchline: Homer's understated retreat to process the news
jokes.explanation: Excellent understated reaction. Homer's casual exit is perfect character work - avoiding confrontation through mundane excuse. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're pregnant? Again?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, Homer. We're going to have another baby.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... another baby... D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I need a donut.

jokes.text: New job? Marge is pregnant?! No!
jokes.setup: Homer processing all the information at once
jokes.punchline: Homer's delayed explosive reaction
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the building tension. Homer's delayed reaction and connecting the job situation to the pregnancy works well.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Oh, just that one time. Actually, when I was about to have Bart... You're pregnant?!
jokes.setup: Homer claiming he only had one tantrum
jokes.punchline: Flashback showing he reacted the same way to Bart's pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure showing Homer's pattern of behavior. Creates a running gag about his consistent reactions.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I only had one tantrum.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, Bart's pregnant?

jokes.text: And then when I told him about Lisa... You're pregnant again?!
jokes.setup: Continuing the pattern of Homer's pregnancy reactions
jokes.punchline: Same reaction to Lisa's pregnancy, establishing the pattern
jokes.explanation: Excellent running gag structure. The 'again' shows escalation and establishes Homer's consistent inability to handle pregnancy news.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not again! Why does this keep happening to me?

jokes.text: That new baby in your mother's womb was a ticking time bomb... threatening to blow this family eight ways from Sunday.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his reaction to the pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Describing an unborn baby as a 'ticking time bomb'
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about the financial impact of children. The military/explosive metaphor is appropriately over-dramatic for Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An unborn baby? That's just a ticking time bomb!

jokes.text: You can't expect a person... to sit for 30 minutes straight. I'm gonna get a snack and maybe go to the bathroom. I'll stay here, but I'm gonna think about products I might like to purchase.
jokes.setup: Long storytelling sequence
jokes.punchline: Breaking the fourth wall about TV commercial expectations and attention spans
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor about television viewing habits and commercial breaks. Lisa's line about thinking about products is particularly sharp media satire.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So anyway, I was telling Lisa how TV commercials are designed to manipulate your attention span...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, studies show that the average viewer loses focus after just 30 seconds of content.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and that's why they keep making them shorter and shorter. Pretty soon they'll just be— (looks directly at camera) —oh wait, we've only been talking for like 2 minutes. Aren't you guys supposed to be watching an ad by now?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: (sighs) Bart, you're breaking the fourth wall again.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I know, but seriously, doesn't anyone else feel like this bit is going on too long?

jokes.text: We're doomed. Doomed, I tells you!
jokes.setup: Homer's reaction to the pregnancy financial implications
jokes.punchline: Over-dramatic proclamation with folksy grammar ('I tells you')
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer over-drama with the grammatical quirk adding character flavor. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I tells you, we're doomed! Doomed! How am I gonna feed another mouth? I can barely afford to feed the ones I got!

jokes.text: Okay, but I know funny.
jokes.setup: Being told to let Homer tell the story
jokes.punchline: Bart's confident assertion of his comedic expertise
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor and character work. Bart's supreme confidence in his comedic judgment is perfect. Very economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey, I know comedy. I've watched TV for like, three hours.

jokes.text: Bowling. Bowling here. Come bowl now. Get your bowling. Who's ready? Bowling.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to drum up business for the bowling alley
jokes.punchline: Treating bowling like a street vendor selling hot dogs
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about inappropriate sales techniques. Homer's desperate marketing approach is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hot dogs! Get your hot dogs! Fresh hot dogs!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I mean... bowling! Get your bowling! Fresh bowling!

jokes.text: My marketing plan attracted a record number of police and fire officials... but few stayed to bowl. I failed.
jokes.setup: Homer's street marketing for bowling
jokes.punchline: The 'customers' were actually emergency responders, Homer's matter-of-fact failure admission
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and deadpan delivery. The implication that his marketing caused emergency responses is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on down to Bowl-a-Rama! We've got lanes, we've got shoes, we've got... uh... stuff!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Homer, we're here because dispatch got a call about a suspicious character loitering in the street.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you're here now! You wanna bowl or what?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Is this a legitimate business?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure. Well, no. Not really. I was just trying to get people to come down there.

dialogue.speaker: Fireman
dialogue.line: We were dispatched for a disturbance.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the only disturbance here is how bad you guys are gonna bowl.

jokes.text: And the way you kept the young people away, that was beautiful.
jokes.setup: Uncle Al praising Homer's work
jokes.punchline: Treating Homer's failure to attract customers as a positive achievement
jokes.explanation: Good ironic praise. Shows how Homer's failure was so complete it became useful in an unexpected way.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm impressed! You've managed to keep customers away from this place.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: Do you know how hard that is? Most businesses try to attract people. You've achieved the opposite.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Al
dialogue.line: That takes real talent, boy. Real talent.

jokes.text: Don't worry, Joey. We'll make it to California someday. Sure, we will, Mr. Homer. Sure, we will.
jokes.setup: Homer's overly emotional goodbye
jokes.punchline: Homer creating fake shared dreams with coworkers, Joey humoring him
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor about Homer's inappropriate emotional investment. Joey's patient response adds to the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna miss you guys so much. Remember that time we all went to Moe's and shared that pitcher of beer?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, that never happened.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it happened in my dreams. And in my dreams, we're best friends.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: We're already best friends, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, I mean REAL best friends. Like in my dream where we all became astronauts and flew to the moon in a giant donut.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That sounds pretty good, Homer.

jokes.text: Boy, this is depressing. I know what'd cheer us up. Bowling.
jokes.setup: Workers feeling sad after Homer leaves
jokes.punchline: Suggesting their workplace activity as entertainment to cheer up
jokes.explanation: Good workplace humor about finding entertainment in your job. Economical and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Bowling Alley Worker 1
dialogue.line: Hey, cheer up! Why don't we go bowling?

dialogue.speaker: Bowling Alley Worker 2
dialogue.line: But we work at a bowling alley.

jokes.text: So come crawling back, eh? Seems like the classy thing to do would be not to call attention to it.
jokes.setup: Burns noting Homer's return
jokes.punchline: Homer's misguided sense of propriety after being insulted
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Homer's misplaced sense of etiquette in an awkward situation. Shows his social confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, you're back. I trust you'll work twice as hard to make up for your absence.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I'm insulted! Well, actually, I'm not insulted. I'm just saying that so you'll think I have feelings.

jokes.text: As punishment for your desertion... it's company policy to give you the plague. Sir, that's the 'plaque.' Yes, the special de-motivational plaque...
jokes.setup: Burns threatening Homer with punishment
jokes.punchline: Confusing plague/plaque, then doubling down on the cruel intent
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay with perfect Burns characterization. The correction leading to 'de-motivational plaque' escalates the cruelty brilliantly.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Release the plague!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, you mean the plaque.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Eh? Oh. Yes, yes, of course. Release the plaque! It's covered in the plague anyway.

jokes.text: Don't forget: You're here forever.
jokes.setup: The demotivational plaque
jokes.punchline: The plaque's message serving as visual reinforcement of Homer's trapped situation
jokes.explanation: Powerful visual callback that reinforces the dark humor. The plaque becomes a recurring visual reminder of Homer's situation.

dialogue.line: The plaque's message serving as visual reinforcement of Homer's trapped situation

jokes.text: My contractions started an hour ago. Just in one ear and out the other with you, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about his problems
jokes.punchline: Marge revealing she's in labor while he's been self-absorbed
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and character work. Marge's exasperated comment about Homer's self-absorption is perfect timing and delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why does everything happen to me? My job stinks, my boss is a jerk, I can't even get a decent beer at Moe's anymore!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm in labor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, everyone's got problems. Now where did I put the remote?

jokes.text: Congratulations. It's a boy. You're truly the catch of the day.
jokes.setup: Doctor announcing the baby's gender
jokes.punchline: Doctor making a fishing pun to Homer
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that doesn't land well. The fishing reference feels forced and doesn't serve the scene or characters.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Congratulations! It's a boy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I'm afraid the baby is going to be a terrible burden on you. But don't worry, you'll have plenty of time to get over it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How long?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Oh, 18 years or so. After that, it's smooth sailing. Of course, by then you'll be dead.

jokes.text: That's the umbilical cord. It's a girl.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently misidentifying the baby's anatomy
jokes.punchline: Doctor's clinical correction of Homer's mistake
jokes.explanation: Mild anatomical humor. Homer's confusion is character-appropriate but the joke is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is the baby gonna be okay, Doc?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes, but I'm afraid your son is going to need a blood transfusion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no! You can have some of mine, boy!

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: That won't be necessary. We have plenty of type O blood on hand.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Type O? I thought he was a boy!

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: He is a boy. Type O is his blood type.

jokes.text: Come on, Marge, show a little enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Homer encouraging Marge to be more excited about the baby
jokes.punchline: Homer asking for enthusiasm after his own 'Okay' response
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic humor and character work. Homer's complete lack of self-awareness about his own reaction is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, Marge, try to sound a little more excited about the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it? That's all the enthusiasm you can muster? I said 'Okay' and I sounded more excited than that!

tmdb_overview: The story of Maggie's birth is told in flashbacks: after Homer quits the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant to pursue his dream of working at a bowling alley, Marge reveals that she is pregnant.
insight: Homer's baby-fear plot sustains 67 jokes across 22 minutes with escalating absurdity.
